slides.eyebrow: Education · Is sync realistic as primary income? The honest answer after 90 days

[01 hook.headline] copy (127 chars) exceeds slot budget (57 at 200px) — needs a rewrite

slides.body:
Compare that to streaming:
1 million streams = roughly $3,000–$4,000 at standard rates.
Getting to 1 million streams for most independent composers: 6–18 months. Sync wins on timeline — if you can get the placements. — — — WHAT MAKES IT VIABLE AS PRIMARY INCOME Critical variables:
→ Catalogue size: 30–60 sync-ready tracks (not 300 rough tracks)
→ Placement rate: one per quarter is sustainable; one per month is scalable
→ PRO registration: every placement must be registered to collect performance income
→ Repeat brief flow: supervisors who return = predictable pipeline — — — THE HONEST LIMITS Sync is not passive income.
Each placement requires documentation, brief response, negotiation, and delivery. It's also not consistent month-to-month.
A quiet month with no briefs is followed by a month with four. What sync offers that streaming doesn't:
— Significantly higher per-use income
— Performance royalty compounding over years
— Industry relationships that generate further work What streaming offers that sync doesn't:
— Volume (thousands of micro-transactions vs. dozens of large ones)
— Speed (release today, earn this month)
— No gatekeeping (no vetting required) — — — THE HONEST ANSWER Yes — with 3+ confirmed placements per year, PRO registration, and a documented catalogue of 40+ tracks, sync can be primary income. No — if you haven't completed rights documentation, don't have stems, or haven't registered with a PRO. The talent is there. The infrastructure has to catch up first. #AfricanComposers #SyncLicensing #SyncIncome #SyncMaster
